data.title: Allspring Special Small Cap Value Fund Q4 2025 Portfolio Review
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the Allspring Special Small Cap Value Fund Q4 2025 Commentary.

Quarterly review

  • The fund (ESPNX) underperformed the Russell 2000 Value Index during the fourth quarter.
  • Stock selection in information technology (IT) and consumer discretionary contributed the most to relative performance.
  • Stock selection in the materials and health care sectors were the largest detractors from relative performance.

Top contributors and detractors to quarter-end fund performance

Contributors

  • Haemonetics Corp. (HAE)
  • Mueller Industries, Inc. (MLI)
  • Globus Medical Inc. (GMED)
  • White Mountains Insurance Group Ltd. (WTM)
  • Mayville Engineering Company, Inc. (MEC)

Detractors

  • Eagle Materials Inc. (EXP)
  • Alamo Group Inc. (ALG)
  • J&J Snack Foods Corp. (JJSF)
  • Janus International Group, Inc. (JBI)
  • TriMas Corp. (TRS)

The holdings identified do not represent all of the securities purchased or sold during the period shown and should not be construed as a recommendation to purchase or sell a particular security. Information on calculation methodology and a list showing the overall contribution of each holding in the account for the period shown are available upon request.

Stock selection in IT and consumer discretionary contributed the most to relative performance versus the Russell 2000 Value Index. The largest contributor was Haemonetics Corp., a medical device company focused on products related to blood collection and management. The company is a market leader in plasma collection machines and has built a strong hospital business through several acquisitions in recent years. The increasing mix of this hospital business, which is higher growth combined with nice margins, should drive cash flow expansion in the coming years, especially as some of the acquired assets continue to reach scale. During the quarter, the company announced stronger-than-expected results, alleviating concerns that had surfaced in their prior print, and the shares quickly regained lost ground. Shares remain attractive from our reward/risk valuation perspective.

Another top contributor was Mueller Industries, Inc., an industrial corporation whose holdings manufacture vital goods for important markets such as air, water, oil, and gas distribution; climate comfort; food preservation; energy transmission; medical; aerospace; and automotive. The stock is beginning to appreciate the durability of the cash flow stream through a full market cycle, in addition to the modest exposure to data center construction that should help support margins for the next several quarters and perhaps years. Its balance sheet has more than $1 billion of net cash that positions Mueller well to continue its strategy of consolidating smaller niche players in the industry while also buying back stock and paying dividends. We continue to hold a substantial position given an attractive reward/risk valuation.

Stock selection in the materials and health care sectors were the largest detractors from relative performance against the index. Within materials, the largest detractor during the quarter was Eagle Materials Inc., a provider of heavy construction materials (cement, aggregates) and light building materials (wallboard) to residential and nonresidential markets. The U.S. housing market remains undersupplied, suggesting that lower rates and a resilient economy should support housing demand long term. Quarterly poor performance was driven by weaker wallboard demand amid macroeconomic headwinds in residential construction, despite resilience in cement and aggregates. Eagle generates substantial free cash flow, maintains an under-levered balance sheet, and has significant opportunity to create shareholder value through disciplined capital deployment. We continue to hold our position and view the reward/risk profile as attractive at current levels.

Another large detractor at the relative portfolio level was Alamo Group Inc. The company engages in the design and manufacture of agricultural equipment and infrastructure maintenance equipment for governmental and industrial use. Shares of the company detracted from performance as weakness in vegetation management continued to offset strength within industrial equipment. In response to ongoing weakness within vegetation, management has consolidated facilities in an effort to reduce fixed costs and improve capacity utilization. Encouragingly, bookings in the division slightly improved during the fourth quarter. We continue to believe the company remains well positioned to deliver shareholder value via capital deployment given its strong balance sheet and cash flow generation. In this regard, late in the quarter, the company announced the acquisition of Petersen Industries, a leading manufacturer of truck-mounted grapple loaders. Given an attractive acquisition multiple for the 25% earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation, and amortization margin business, we believe the deal is well aligned with the new CEO’s strategy for bolt-on mergers and acquisitions.

BENCHMARK DESCRIPTIONS:

The Lipper averages are compiled by Lipper, Inc., an independent mutual fund research and rating service. Each Lipper average represents a universe of funds that are similar in investment objective. You cannot invest directly in a Lipper average.

The Morningstar Category average is the average return for the peer group based on the returns of each individual fund within the group. The total return of the Morningstar Category average does not include the effect of sales charges. You cannot invest directly in a Morningstar Category average.

The Russell 2000® Value Index measures the performance of those Russell 2000 companies with lower price/book ratios and lower forecasted growth values. You cannot invest directly in an index.

Diversification does not ensure or guarantee better performance and cannot eliminate the risk of investment losses.

ATTRIBUTION ANALYSIS:

Performance attribution and sector returns are calculated using the Brinson-Fachler attribution model. As such, performance attribution calculations may differ from the fund’s actual investment results. Common sources of attribution dispersion from official returns include: the use of end of day holdings and prices, the exclusion of fees, and the use of myriad pricing sources to accommodate different benchmark providers.

DEFINITION OF TERMS:

Alpha measures the difference between a fund’s actual returns and its expected performance given its level of risk (as measured by beta).

Beta measures fund volatility relative to general market movements. It is a standardized measure of systematic risk in comparison with a specified index. The benchmark beta is 1.00 by definition.

Downside capture measures a fund’s replication of its benchmark during periods of negative returns. During periods of negative benchmark returns, a downside capture ratio less than 100% reflects product performance greater than the benchmark and a downside capture ratio greater than 100% reflects performance less than the benchmark.

Information ratio measures the consistency of excess return (return in excess of a benchmark). This value is determined by taking the annualized excess return over a benchmark (style benchmark by default) and dividing it by the standard deviation of excess return.

R-squared is a measurement of how similar a fund’s historical performance has been to that of the benchmark. The measure ranges from 0.0, which means that the fund’s performance bears no relationship to the performance of the index, to 1.0, which means that the fund’s performance was perfectly synchronized with the performance of the benchmark.

Sharpe ratio measures the potential reward offered by a mutual fund relative to its risk level. The ratio uses a fund’s standard deviation and its excess return to determine reward per unit of risk. The higher the Sharpe ratio, the better the fund’s historical risk-adjusted performance.

Standard deviation represents the degree to which an investment’s performance has varied from its average performance over a particular time period.

Tracking error measures the extent to which a manager’s performance mimics that of a benchmark. The value is the standard deviation of the difference between a fund’s performance and a benchmark’s performance.

Upside capture measures a fund’s replication of its benchmark during periods of positive returns. During periods of positive benchmark returns, an upside capture ratio greater than 100% reflects product performance greater than the benchmark and an upside capture ratio less than 100% reflects performance less than the benchmark.

Risks: Investing involves risk, including the possible loss of principal. Stock values fluctuate in response to the activities of individual companies and general market and economic conditions. Smaller company stocks tend to be more volatile and less liquid than those of larger companies. Consult the fund’s prospectus for additional information on these and other risks.

The Morningstar Rating for funds, or star rating, is calculated for managed products (including mutual funds, variable annuity and variable life subaccounts, exchange-traded funds, closed-end funds, and separate accounts) with at least a three-year history. Exchange-traded funds and open-ended mutual funds are considered a single population for comparative purposes. It is calculated based on a Morningstar risk-adjusted return measure that accounts for variation in a managed product's monthly excess performance, placing more emphasis on downward variations and rewarding consistent performance. The Morningstar Rating does not include any adjustment for sales loads. The top 10% of products in each product category receive 5 stars, the next 22.5% receive 4 stars, the next 35% receive 3 stars, the next 22.5% receive 2 stars, and the bottom 10% receive 1 star. The Overall Morningstar Rating for a managed product is derived from a weighted average of the performance figures associated with its 3-, 5-, and 10-year (if applicable) Morningstar Rating metrics. The weights are: 100% 3-year rating for 36–59 months of total returns, 60% 5-year rating/40% 3-year rating for 60–119 months of total returns, and 50% 10-year rating/30% 5-year rating/20% 3-year rating for 120 or more months of total returns. While the 10-year overall star rating formula seems to give the most weight to the 10-year period, the most recent 3-year period actually has the greatest impact because it is included in all three rating periods.

Across U.S.-domiciled small value funds, the Special Small Cap Value Fund received 2 stars among 462 funds, 2 stars among 439 funds, and 3 stars among 362 funds for the 3-, 5-, and 10-year periods, respectively. data.title: SharkNinja: Still A Buy As Market Share Gains Defy Macro Weakness
data.description:
Introduction

The last time I covered SharkNinja (SN), I covered their excellent performance, ramping up aggressively since their 2023 spin-off, being able to grow their brands while gaining significant market share and momentum.

Following yet another strong quarter and continued expansion plans, I'm reiterating SN's Buy rating, as the company remains in a very solid financial position and should be able to capitalize on the significant amount of data they gathered to take advantage of a potential consumer recovery.

Internal Developments

SN reported yet another strong quarter, beating the market's EPS and revenue estimates while delivering their 11th consecutive quarter of double-digit top-line growth despite the weak environment they play in. As we can see, their OCF grew extremely well, up nearly 42% compared to 2024, while the cash used for investing activities (mostly their CAPEX) remained almost the same, getting to a free cash flow of at least $474.35 million.

As for their guidance, the company expects a ~$40 million jump in CAPEX at midpoint, while their net sales would continue growing by 10% to 11% (vs. 15.7% in 2025) and the adjusted EBITDA would grow by an even better 11.8% to 12.7% (vs. 19.4% in 2025), while also announcing a $750 million buyback program - 4.33% yield, no expiration date.

Despite this weak environment, the company continues to focus on aggressively winning market share and innovating, with a goal of 25 annual new product introductions, with proven experience in delivering very strong market share growth globally and the ability to utilize their two brands to scale more and more branches (such as beauty recently).

Financially, based on SN's latest report, we can see a very strong position, with current assets covering their total liabilities very well, with a strong $777.29 million worth of cash and cash equivalents on the balance sheet and an ~11.4% increase in inventories.

Again, the equity is significantly smaller than the market cap, but it's important to note that this company's core assets are not physical, relying on their brands instead of actually owning the "heavy" production facilities, and I mentioned back in the previous coverage the gradual decoupling from Joyoung (the chairman's other company), reducing their cap rate significantly over time while he kept reducing his stake, which should be an improvement to their overall image for the market.

Macro Developments

Given the recent tariff reset following the Supreme Court's decision and Trump's subsequent increase to 15%, we could also expect a tailwind from this, as the company's guidance for 2026 assumes minimum tariff rates of 20% for China and Vietnam and 19% for others, although this continues to be a volatile and unpredictable situation.

Meanwhile, we can see potential pressure on the broader markets and consumers coming from the attack against Iran, the situation in Japan, and other factors mentioned before, which have the potential to lead to more weakness, fuel cost increases, general inflation, etc., coming at an already weak time for the consumer.

For example, virtually everyone has been reporting significant weakness in the US consumer environment, with consumer confidence plummeting to the weakest level since 2014 recently despite modest improvements in February 2026, while trade-downs can be seen in everything from the auto industry to apparel and even the most basic needs such as food.

Yet, despite all of these, the company continued to outperform and scale, with their CEO mentioning the following during their Q4 Earnings Call:

Importantly, this performance came against a challenging macro backdrop. While our markets remained under pressure throughout 2025 on top of declines in both '24 and '23, we continue to rapidly gain share. According to Circana, the total U.S. market that we participate in declined in the low single digits year-over-year for the full year 2025, excluding SharkNinja's performance. Q4 was even more challenging for the industry with mid-single-digit declines year-over-year, again, excluding SharkNinja. The magnitude of our outperformance is noteworthy with market share gains across each of our 4 category groupings in '25, Cleaning, Cooking and Beverage, Food Preparation and Beauty and Home Environment.

To be fair, these trade-downs could actually benefit SN to some degree, as they are ultimately known for their great price-quality ratio, offering solid value for the price they come at, while the very high amount of data and knowledge they can gain from their rise in market share can offer important advantages that can allow them to adapt to consumer trends and "capture" the consumer with adjusted price points or products that fit their changing needs in an improving environment.

In fact, the company's CEO even said something regarding this during their Q4 Earnings Call, which is indeed a massive competitive advantage, in my opinion:

Our objective is faster, more precise product innovation and improved messaging and content to resonate in a stronger way with consumers. This is the SharkNinja flywheel. Consumer insights, a resilient global supply chain, always-on marketing and omnichannel distribution. We believe it's a competitive advantage that exceedingly difficult to replicate at our scale. The diversification shows up very clearly in our financial results, sustained double-digit growth, expanding margins and strong free cash flow.

Risks Reiterated

Ultimately, despite the company's strong performance, they'll continue to depend on the environment, and there are certainly scenarios where the demand for their products would fall alongside worsening economic conditions, renewed tariff pressure, and more.

Meanwhile, we shouldn't rule out the competitive pressure, as there's always potential to see another competitor do what SharkNinja did to the giants that ruled the market for so long, even though the company could also buy these if they are agile enough.

Valuation

For SN's DCF to Equity valuation, if we assume a similar midpoint increase of 12.25% for their OCF (same as their Adjusted EBITDA increase), minus the midpoint CAPEX of $200 million, getting us to a modest FCF of $511.8 million in 2026, without assuming any tailwind from tariffs falling. Note that I won't account for any net borrowings, as I'll assume that the company doesn't need to worry about repaying/issuing debt given their strong balance sheet, allowing us to capture their underlying potential.

I'll continue to assume a CAGR of 17.5%, which is not far from the market's EPS expectations (which I believe to be quite conservative given the potential margin improvements thanks to economies of scale) and not unlikely given their strong market position, significant momentum, data and ability to adapt, plus buybacks improving the final numbers on a per share basis, followed by a 10% CAGR for the subsequent 5 years and a 3.5% terminal growth rate given their global operations and potential to focus on higher-potential markets like SEA, with a 10% discount rate to take into account a decent margin of safety despite their strong position and momentum, and 143.5 million shares outstanding based on their 2026 guidance.

This way, we get to an equity value of $15.34 billion, for an intrinsic value of $106.90 per share, which is below the current $122.87, still trading within a fair value range, while their FCF has significant potential to improve, especially in a better environment.

Conclusions

I reiterate SN's Buy rating, as the company continues to expand aggressively and remains in an excellent financial position, allowing them to take advantage of a potential turnaround in the industry.

Although they are trading somewhere in line with my fair value estimate, I'll note that this Buy rating is backed by their strong position and the other advantages named above, while their growth can easily beat these assumptions since we're talking about massive top-line growth that can lead to even better cash flow growth - which they've proven so far - as they scale and build their brands.

There are risks to keep in mind given the industries they play in, but they should be able to use their current momentum and data to adapt to the environment and continue their expansion - potentially in more industries as well.

data.title: Middle East Conflict: Implications For Global Markets
data.description:
By Jason Vaillancourt, CFA

Recent U.S. and Israeli strikes in Iran mark a significant escalation in the Middle East conflict, raising the risk of broader regional spillovers with global market consequences. For investors, the central concern is whether the conflict disrupts energy flows through the Strait of Hormuz, a critical artery for global oil and liquefied natural gas (LNG) supply. A sustained disruption would lift oil prices, increase inflation risks, and alter interest rate expectations. Oil has risen to around $72 a barrel as of March 2, alongside gains in gold and the U.S. dollar. While Iran may lack the capacity to enforce a prolonged full closure, even partial disruptions—via shipping delays, higher insurance costs, or attacks on regional energy infrastructure—could keep prices elevated. The conflict in the Middle East remains a fluid and rapidly evolving situation. We are closely monitoring developments and will continue to provide updates as events unfold.

Key points

  • Conflict exceeds prior containment assumptions. Recent U.S. and Israeli strikes within Iran appear oriented toward regime-level objectives rather than limited deterrence, signaling a materially different and more expansive conflict than last year.
  • Energy supply risks drive the macroeconomic narrative. The dominant economic risk centers on potential disruption to the Strait of Hormuz, a critical maritime chokepoint along Iran’s southern coast. An extended closure could propel oil prices toward $100 per barrel and significantly raise global inflation expectations.
  • Oil-driven inflation would complicate monetary policy. A sustained rise in energy prices would add upside pressure to inflation and interest rates, particularly in the U.S., where $100 oil could push CPI back toward 4%, undermining the recent disinflation trend.
  • Asia faces the greatest exposure, but buffers limit near-term shock. Although OPEC spare capacity (while limited and exists only from Saudi Arabia) and strategic petroleum reserves could partially offset lost Iranian supply, Asia remains most vulnerable to prolonged disruption given its heavy dependence on oil and LNG that transits through the Strait of Hormuz.
  • Markets are pricing tail risk rather than a base-case disruption. While Iran may lack the capacity to sustain a full closure of the Strait of Hormuz even partial disruptions—through higher vessel insurance costs, shipping delays, or targeted infrastructure attacks—could keep energy prices elevated and market volatility high.

Implications for investors

This is a fluid, rapidly moving and expanding conflict. As such, we will be monitoring the unfolding events closely and sharing updates. There are three critical figures that we are closely watching: the U.S. dollar, the interest rate on the 10-year U.S. Treasury note and oil prices.

  • U.S. dollar. As expected, the U.S. dollar is slightly stronger to start the week, but less so than we might have normally expected in a flight to quality amid heightened geopolitical risks. The Japanese yen and Swiss franc are showing strength as perceived safe havens.
  • Oil prices. U.S. near-term oil futures spiked to around $72 on the morning of March 2, while longer-dated contracts (extending to early 2027) moved far less, rising by only a couple of dollars. The strategically vital Strait of Hormuz—through which roughly one quarter of global oil and about one fifth of LNG flows, including a significant share of China’s supply—is effectively closed as Iran’s leadership adopts a more defensive posture. Major shipping firms have suspended transits through the Strait and rerouted vessels around the southern tip of Africa. A potentially prolonged disruption to global trade through key maritime corridors such as the Strait of Hormuz remains a central concern.
  • 10-year U.S. Treasury note. Relative to a week ago, the yield curve remains largely unchanged. The primary channel through which current developments are likely to affect financial markets is via higher expected energy costs and their implications for future inflation, which could constrain the Federal Reserve’s ability to continue cutting rates. We are therefore focused on monitoring key data and evolving risk factors rather than relying on historical traditional playbooks, which have become less reliable given the complexity and extraordinary nature of the current conflict. The range of potential outcomes remains wide, with significant uncertainty around the ultimate trajectory. Notably, there is still a risk of Iranian strikes on energy infrastructure across Gulf Cooperation Council (GCC) countries.

Looking forward

For now, financial market movements remain relatively modest as investors adopt a wait-and-see approach. Risk appetite has held up well despite recent challenges. History suggests that while geopolitical shocks can trigger short-term market moves, a sustained impact typically requires a meaningful shift in underlying economic fundamentals.

Markets have often shown an ability to look through geopolitical episodes, particularly when fundamentals such as corporate earnings and economic growth remain supportive. These conditions continue to underpin market resilience. That said, close attention will be required in the coming days and weeks as events evolve and potential second-order and longer-term unintended consequences become clearer.

Disclosures

Geopolitical events — including military actions — may cause unexpected market volatility, disrupt commodity supply chains, and materially affect the performance of oil-related assets and emerging market securities. These risks may evolve rapidly and may not be fully reflected in market prices. Commodity investments may be affected by the overall market and industry- and commodity-specific factors, and may be more volatile and less liquid than other investments. Investments in a narrowly focused sector such as energy may exhibit higher volatility than investments with broader objectives. Market risk may affect a single issuer, sector of the economy, industry or the market as a whole. Market review

During the quarter, U.S. equity investors digested easing inflation data, a softening labor market, and some caution around the artificial intelligence (AI) buildout. The low-quality rally that began in April continued through the first few weeks of the quarter but did show some signs of fatigue as the quarter progressed, which is common in phase two of a bear market recovery. As risk appetites are more balanced and investors become more discerning, the headwinds our process faced earlier in the recovery have lessened. As we move into the third phase of the recovery, we expect companies with durable asset bases, flexible balance sheets, and sustainable free cash flow to be outperformers versus the benchmark like we have seen in past cycles. The fund underperformed the first two weeks of the quarter but outperformed from mid-October until the end of the quarter as risk appetites moderated.

The Russell 2000 Value Index was the second-best performing of the nine Russell style boxes (behind Large Cap Value) and returned 3.26% for the quarter. Within the index, the health care (+20%), materials (+10%), and communication services (+10%) sectors were the top performers. Meanwhile, IT (-5%) and consumer staples (-4%) were the worst-performing sectors within the index.

The company is a market leader in plasma collection machines and has built a strong hospital business through several acquisitions in recent years. The increasing mix of this hospital business, which is higher growth combined with nice margins, should drive cash flow expansion in the coming years, especially as some of the acquired assets continue to reach scale. During the quarter, the company announced stronger-than-expected results, alleviating concerns that had surfaced in their prior print, and the shares quickly regained lost ground. Shares remain attractive from our reward/risk valuation perspective.

Another top contributor was Mueller Industries, Inc., an industrial corporation whose holdings manufacture vital goods for important markets such as air, water, oil, and gas distribution; climate comfort; food preservation; energy transmission; medical; aerospace; and automotive. The stock is beginning to appreciate the durability of the cash flow stream through a full market cycle, in addition to the modest exposure to data center construction that should help support margins for the next several quarters and perhaps years. Its balance sheet has more than $1 billion of net cash that positions Mueller well to continue its strategy of consolidating smaller niche players in the industry while also buying back stock and paying dividends. We continue to hold a substantial position given an attractive reward/risk valuation.

The U.S. housing market remains undersupplied, suggesting that lower rates and a resilient economy should support housing demand long term. Quarterly poor performance was driven by weaker wallboard demand amid macroeconomic headwinds in residential construction, despite resilience in cement and aggregates. Eagle generates substantial free cash flow, maintains an under-levered balance sheet, and has significant opportunity to create shareholder value through disciplined capital deployment. We continue to hold our position and view the reward/risk profile as attractive at current levels.

Another large detractor at the relative portfolio level was Alamo Group Inc. The company engages in the design and manufacture of agricultural equipment and infrastructure maintenance equipment for governmental and industrial use. Shares of the company detracted from performance as weakness in vegetation management continued to offset strength within industrial equipment. In response to ongoing weakness within vegetation, management has consolidated facilities in an effort to reduce fixed costs and improve capacity utilization. Encouragingly, bookings in the division slightly improved during the fourth quarter. We continue to believe the company remains well positioned to deliver shareholder value via capital deployment given its strong balance sheet and cash flow generation. In this regard, late in the quarter, the company announced the acquisition of Petersen Industries, a leading manufacturer of truck-mounted grapple loaders. Given an attractive acquisition multiple for the 25% earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation, and amortization margin business, we believe the deal is well aligned with the new CEO’s strategy for bolt-on mergers and acquisitions.

Fund positioning and outlook

The fund made minor changes to positioning during the three-month period. The fund increased its weight in the IT and consumer discretionary sectors while reducing its weight in the financials and industrials sectors as reward/risk ratios dictated. The fund’s largest overweights versus its benchmark remained industrials and materials. The largest underweights were real estate and financials. These weights were driven by our bottom-up reward/risk process as well as our portfolio construction methodology that is designed to isolate active risk to security selection as much as possible.

The low-quality rally that began in early April started to show signs of fatigue in the fourth quarter. This is not uncommon for the first phase following a bear market correction. The correction, which started near the end of the first quarter of 2025 and ended a few weeks later upon the "90-day pause," was very brief by bear market standards but nonetheless a correction. We expect the traditional cycle dynamics to continue as we move past phase one of the recovery and into phase two, which is characterized by return to quality factors such as earnings predictability, low financial leverage, and higher return-on-equity firms outperforming the benchmark.

The U.S. economy remains in decent shape and will be supported by the fiscal stimulus embedded in the latest reconciliation bill passed this summer. However, the current cycle is not without risks as investors monitor the weakening labor market. Fortunately, the Federal Reserve has the capacity and the resolve to react accordingly if unemployment rises. As that risk is realized, credit spreads will likely widen, lower beta stocks should lead, and companies with strong balance sheet positions should become more highly valued than they are today.

Since the start of COVID, the market has followed the oscillations of fiscal, monetary, and geopolitical changes by rapidly flipping from risk-on to risk-off behavior. What has been lost is the "normal" middle that traditionally makes up the longest part of the market and economic cycle. We believe that "normal" is simply a matter of knowing the direction of travel (fiscal and monetary) and the rules of the game (political and regulatory). With these questions being largely answered as we wrap up 2025, we expect "normal" is around the corner and the merits of owning competitively advantaged businesses that produce strong and growing free cash flow will become in vogue and the beta-chasing phase of this cycle will be behind us.

We expect the traditional cycle dynamics to continue as we move past phase one of the recovery and into phase two, which is characterized with a return to quality factors.

The first leg of the AI revolution has been focused on the neoclouds/graphics processing units/semis/memory and large-cap growth, but similar to past “revolutionary” advancements, the capital spending around the infrastructure buildout will benefit many other companies and industries, many of which fall within the value universes. The 2000–2010 decade saw a handoff from growth to value as the internet revolution buildout evolved. The speculative/non-earners have been the early strong performers in small and mid; however, investors will become more discerning and demand a return on invested capital from companies. Active management will be essential to determine who the winners and losers will be in this “sorting phase.”

DEFINITION OF TERMS:

Alpha measures the difference between a fund’s actual returns and its expected performance given its level of risk (as measured by beta).

Beta measures fund volatility relative to general market movements. It is a standardized measure of systematic risk in comparison with a specified index. The benchmark beta is 1.00 by definition.

Downside capture measures a fund’s replication of its benchmark during periods of negative returns. During periods of negative benchmark returns, a downside capture ratio less than 100% reflects product performance greater than the benchmark and a downside capture ratio greater than 100% reflects performance less than the benchmark.

Information ratio measures the consistency of excess return (return in excess of a benchmark). This value is determined by taking the annualized excess return over a benchmark (style benchmark by default) and dividing it by the standard deviation of excess return.

R-squared is a measurement of how similar a fund’s historical performance has been to that of the benchmark. The measure ranges from 0.0, which means that the fund’s performance bears no relationship to the performance of the index, to 1.0, which means that the fund’s performance was perfectly synchronized with the performance of the benchmark.

Sharpe ratio measures the potential reward offered by a mutual fund relative to its risk level. The ratio uses a fund’s standard deviation and its excess return to determine reward per unit of risk. The higher the Sharpe ratio, the better the fund’s historical risk-adjusted performance.

Standard deviation represents the degree to which an investment’s performance has varied from its average performance over a particular time period.

Tracking error measures the extent to which a manager’s performance mimics that of a benchmark. The value is the standard deviation of the difference between a fund’s performance and a benchmark’s performance.

Upside capture measures a fund’s replication of its benchmark during periods of positive returns. During periods of positive benchmark returns, an upside capture ratio greater than 100% reflects product performance greater than the benchmark and an upside capture ratio less than 100% reflects performance less than the benchmark.

data.title: Marine War Insurance For Hormuz Dries Up As Middle East War Intensifies
data.description:
Ships wanting to pass through the Strait of Hormuz are finding it almost impossible to buy hull war cover following the conflict between Iran and the US and Israel.

The Strait, a narrow stretch of water connecting the Persian Gulf and the Gulf of Oman, is a key shipping lane through which roughly 20% of the world's seaborne oil and gas is transported.

"If you went to the hull market right now and said: 'I've got a tanker going through the Strait of Hormuz,' I think there is a possibility that you would struggle to find… underwriters looking to write that," David Smith, head of marine at insurance broker McGill & Partners, said in an interview.

The US and Israel on Feb. 28 began a sustained air campaign against Iran, which has responded with missile and drone attacks against both military targets and energy infrastructure in the Persian Gulf.

Iran has also threatened to close the Strait and said it will attack ships attempting to traverse it. While Iran's naval capabilities have been seriously degraded and few ships have come under actual fire, the Strait is already "de-facto closed given that a lot of insurance companies have stopped insuring transiting vessels," Bilal Bassiouni, head of risk forecasting at risk advisory firm Pangea-Risk, said in an interview.

"No major carriers are transiting the Strait," he added.

Price spike

For vessels travelling to the Persian Gulf that are not planning to go through the Strait, the price of hull war cover has jumped. While noting that prices are "highly subjective" and based on criteria such as vessel size, flag, ownership and cargo, Smith is seeing "rates of up to 1% [of the ship's value] for seven days without breaching the Strait of Hormuz."

Hull war insurance prices for the Gulf were around 0.25% of a ship's value before the fighting broke out, according to several news reports.

Shipowners also face paying more for liability cover if their vessels are travelling to the Gulf. Protection and indemnity (P&I) clubs — mutual marine insurers that provide ships' liability cover — have been cancelling certain war cover. All 12 members of the International Group of P&I Clubs, a risk pool that covers 90% of the world's ocean-going tonnage, have given 72 hours' notice of cancellation of parts of their war cover in the Gulf.

The cancellations are for non-poolable war risks, which cannot be shared between the International Group members.

"In most cases the clubs will be offering to reinstate war coverage at terms to be agreed," Peter Hulyer, head of P&I for the UK at insurance broker Marsh LLC, said in a press statement. "Mutual P&I cover offered by the clubs is unaffected."

The cancelled cover is typically related to war risks included the clubs' charterers' coverage, a range of covers offered to those chartering vessels. The move brings the P&I war market into line with the hull war market, Smith said.

Waiting game

The situation in the Strait of Hormuz means that ships are now building up in the area and looking for alternative ports, Smith said.

"The underwriters are waiting to see what happens, and I think most sensible shipowners are waiting to see," he said. "No shipowner wants to put either his asset, and more importantly, his crew in danger. They'll look for every alternative other than do that."

Docking at ports in the Gulf could also be risky. There have been attacks on the Port of Duqm in Oman, which Bassiouni said signals "a higher risk of collateral damage around other vessels that may be anchored in ports close to what Iran may deem as legitimate military targets in those waters."

Obstructing the Strait of Hormuz could trap some ships in the Persian Gulf, which could trigger so-called blocking and trapping claims under war policies, Smith said. However, vessels would need to be stuck for six months, a year or longer for this coverage to come into play, Smith said, "so that concern at the moment will be right at the very back of most underwriters' thoughts."

Red Sea risks

While Hormuz has been a focal point for marine insurers in the early stages of the war, they are also alert to the potential for the Houthis, an Iran-backed group based in Yemen, to resume attacks on vessels in the Red Sea. "Everyone is keeping a very, very watchful eye also right now on the Red Sea," Smith said.

Trouble in the Red Sea does not cause the same level of disruption as Hormuz because there are alternative routes available. Even so, an underwriter being asked for a price to go through the Red Sea now would give it more careful consideration than they would a week ago, Smith said. "We all need to be very concerned about what's going on within the Persian Gulf," he said. "But I think also you still need to keep half an eye on the Red Sea as well."

The level of threat from the Houthis will depend on how long the war lasts, according to Bassiouni. Following the 2025 ceasefire deal between the Houthis and the US, the Houthis' calculations will be more based on domestic considerations and their relationship with the US than Iran, he said.

That said, as airstrikes against Iran continue, the Houthis may eventually decide to "reactivate the Red Sea attacks to shift pressure away from Iran and also increase leverage that they may have at the same time," Bassiouni said.

data.title: SoFi: The Rebound Has Started (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Reasons to upgrade SOFI

Sharing a bearish thesis on SoFi Technologies (SOFI) in early December was a bold move because the stock was on fire at that time, which made my earlier bearish articles look not very good. However, the market sentiment has shifted towards more defensive stance and investors started repricing all aggressive growth stories in 2026. Thus, SOFI has been down by more than 30% since early December. After such a sharp drop, SOFI's valuation now looks much better than it did in late 2025. The company's PEG ratio has dropped to 0.79, significantly lower compared to the mature financial services sector's multiple of 0.97.

SOFI's low PEG ratio implies that it has an extremely bullish forward P/E ratio setup. If we delve into detail in terms of forward P/E, we can see that the projected trajectory of P/E compression is reasonable as it factors in aggressive EPS growth deceleration as large numbers kick in. For a rapidly growing business like SOFI, the estimated forward P/E for FY2027 of just 23 looks quite attractive. For example, in my previous SOFI article I highlighted that the projected FY2027 forward P/E was as high as 35 in early December.

I think that SOFI currently looks much more attractive because there were no disappointments from the fundamentals side. The company's Q4 earnings were stellar with another dual beat. Quarterly revenue surpassed $1 billion for the first time in history, increasing by impressive 37% YoY. Bottom line profitability continues improving as well, underpinned by excellent results in critical business metrics (members and products). Finally, the company also provided an upbeat guidance. Thus, fundamentals remain extremely strong while the share price has dropped significantly.

Moreover, the stock recently rebounded sharply from the oversold territory, which might indicate that we are witnessing early innings of a solid share price rebound. The rebound is likely to be supported by the fact that Wall Street analysts maintain their consensus target price of approximately $26-27 with no downgrades despite the recent sharp share price drop. It indicates that SOFI's long-term bullish case remains rock-solid. SOFI is currently undervalued by 42% if the share price is compared to the average Wall Street target price.

Furthermore, in my previous bearish call I highlighted quite aggressive insider selling as a warning sign. The situation changed in 2026 after the share price started tanking with a few notable insider buys recently. The latest one is a $1 million worth purchase made by the CEO, Anthony Noto. He bought 56k shares at a weighted average price of $17.88, and it was absorbed by the market with optimism. I tend to agree with this optimism because in November, the CEO was selling SOFI aggressively. This pivot might indicate that he believes SOFI is extremely attractively valued at the moment.

Robust secular bullish case for the entire fintech industry appears to remain intact. One of the global fintech leaders, Revolut, continues driving aggressive growth like SOFI. It is a larger player since it operates across major European economies while SOFI is focused only on the domestic market. In the current geopolitical environment of deteriorating relationships between the U.S. and its historical allies and due to President Trump's "America First" motto, I do not expect Revolut to expand into the U.S. market any time soon. However, it is very useful to keep an eye over Revolut's performance and plans to assess the power of fintech-related tailwinds because generational shift is applicable to the whole world. Recent report from European Business Magazine suggests that Revolut is likely to go public this year with a potential valuation of $150 billion, which will be a significant increase compared to previous valuation rounds. I believe that it is a solid validation supporting structural tailwinds for all rapidly growing fintech players including SOFI.

Finally, I think that betting on SOFI's rapid recovery is a safer bet than trying to buy the SaaSpocalypse dip. SOFI's attractiveness amid the current AI-related panic is that it is disrupting and rapidly growing in a mature and stable financial services industry. Generative AI companies can potentially disrupt various areas spanning software, digital advertising, or content creation. However, I think that it is almost impossible to imagine that OpenAI or Anthropic will ever apply for a bank charter and become a competitor to SOFI. What I mean here is that this distinction is highly likely obvious not only to me but to other investors as well. Therefore, sentiment around SOFI is likely to improve faster compared to major beaten-down software stocks.

Reasons against upgrading SOFI

The current geopolitical environment doesn't favor investing in growth companies. A big part of investors is currently obsessed with hydrocarbons and precious metals as there are various large wars simultaneously in different part. The U.S. is the largest oil and gas producer, but when other large players like major Gulf producers or Russia are involved in a war, such sentiment is understandable. Investors are currently fearful, and it is not a good setup for stocks like SOFI despite stellar fundamentals.

From the business perspective, there is an uncertainty regarding the effect of falling Bitcoin price on SOFI's earnings long-term growth potential. The company has been introducing various crypto-related products over the last several quarters. The management did not disclose the expected effect from crypto-related business on SOFI's earnings. However, since SOFI's focus is rendering financial services, it is obvious that the success of crypto-related services will depend on volume of transactions. With Bitcoin prices tanking in 2026, the interest from retail investors is likely to cool down. If major cryptocurrencies continue tanking, it might adversely affect SOFI's earnings revisions.

And even if the management did not expect rapid returns from crypto-related initiatives, they are likely expected to help maintain aggressive growth in number of members by attracting more people interested in digital assets. With that being said, even if there is no effect on earnings revisions, SOFI's actual member growth figures might be slower than expected.

The verdict

There are a few solid reasons to start buying SOFI despite the current highly uncertain environment. Fundamentals remain rock-solid, and the CEO's fresh insider buy might indicate that the stock is really extremely undervalued with notable upward earnings revisions ahead. Moreover, a sharp rebound out of the oversold territory means that a big rally might be starting and SOFI merits an upgrade to Buy.

data.title: U.S.-Israel Iran War Provokes Shipping Lane Shifts
data.description:
The US and Israel on Feb. 28 launched a large-scale, coordinated air campaign against Iran, striking a broad range of leadership, military, security and nuclear targets. A forced government change is now a key objective, according to S&P Global Market Intelligence country risk analysts.

Global supply networks may feel the impact through a mixture of energy market disruptions, airfreight challenges and container freight shipping network interruptions. In the case of energy, shipping via the Strait of Hormuz needs to continue; flows of LNG may be disrupted as well as crude oil.

Energy supply chain disruption

Absent an extended closure of the Strait or the destruction of liquefaction assets, the impact is unlikely to be long-term in nature. The Islamic Revolution Guard Corps (IRGC) is likely to expand targeting of critical Gulf energy infrastructure if US and Israeli strikes target Iranian critical national infrastructure and major crude export terminals.

Air freight disruption

Global air freight networks face challenges from the halt to flights through many of the regional ports, including the hubs of Doha and Dubai. These hubs handle around 2.6 million metric tons and 2.2 million metric tons of airfreight, respectively, or around 4.0% of the total global airfreight volumes.

The ability of air freight networks to adapt is partly limited by aircraft flight ranges, though networks can rapidly adapt, as was shown during the pandemic.

Container shipping disruption

Container shipping faces challenges to both local actions in the Strait of Hormuz and the wider region through shipping via the Red Sea.

Local actions in the Strait of Hormuz impact key shipping hubs for container freight, including Jebel Ali in Dubai, as we previously identified at the time of June 2025 conflict.

Container lines are also redirecting shipping away from the Red Sea once more. CMA CGM SA has ordered all vessels in the Gulf to proceed to shelter and AP Moeller Maersk A/S has rerouted vessels bound for the Red Sea around the Cape.

data.title: With Energy Volatility, Buy MPLX For Reliability
data.description:
Introduction

MPLX (MPLX) has been a staple in my portfolio for a while, and with the recent events that transpired, I believe that it is important to review my thesis on the company. With new earnings out and geopolitical shifts in the Middle East, I’m digging into whether the company can ride on those tailwinds.

Current Dynamics

I’ll first quickly go through the latest earnings to understand the recent dynamics for the MLP. It was a solid beat and raise story as the partnership continues to be a cash flow machine, thanks to MPLX’s dominance in the Permian and Marcellus. EPU stood at $1.17, a beat of 12 cents, though that is not really the metric that we look at with MLPs. EBITDA rose to $1.8B compared with $1.76B in Q4 2024. Looking at the top-line, it reached $3.25B for the quarter, a slight beat of $70MM and a 6.2% Y/Y improvement. MPLX declared a distribution of $1.0765, giving the company a forward yield of 7.64%.

For the full year, net income attributable to the partnership stood at $4.912B, a drastic Y/Y increase compared to $4.357B reported in the preceding fiscal year. This profitability level translated directly into exceptionally strong core operating metrics. EBITDA reached an unprecedented $7.017B for the year.

For 2026, MPLX isn’t just sitting on its cash, mangament announced a $2.7B capital plan for 2026, which is split into $2.4B for growth and $300MM for maintenance. The company is effectively doubling down on the wellhead to water strategy, specifically expanding the BANGL pipeline and building new processing plants to capture the massive associated gas coming out of West Texas.

In my opinion, the company is currently in a boring but great phase, considering its strong balance sheet, its incredible utilization rate thanks to Marathon Petroleum (MPC), and enough free cash flow to fund growth while still raising distribution to unitholders.

I’ll now touch on the Middle East situation as I believe that it will have a real impact on MPLX’s business. For a domestic midstream giant, the impact is a double-edged sword as it creates massive tailwinds for US energy production, but it will also introduce volatility into the NGL markets that MPLX is currently betting on. With the Strait of Hormuz being effectively closed, roughly 20% of the world’s oil and LNG supply is offline. This will pivot the world’s eyes toward the Permian Basin and the Marcellus/Utica, which are areas where MPLX is currently dominating. High global prices will incentivize US producers to pump at maximum capacity, and with the fee-based model, more volumes moving through their pipes equals simply more cash flow.

More so, MPLX’s 2026 capital plan is 90% focused on Natural Gas and NGL services. The Iran conflict has hit Qatar’s LNG exports, causing a global gas shock. With Middle Eastern NGL exports disrupted, US NGLs become the global fallback. The expansion of the BANGL pipeline and the Secretariat processing plants becomes even more strategic. High international prices make the mid-teens returns they promised in their Q4 call look easily achievable.

Though there are things to watch out for, considering the inherent volatility of the situation. As MPC is a refiner, if crude prices spike too high, the crack spread could get squeezed because it costs more to buy the raw oil. But I’ll argue that US refiners often have an advantage during Middle East turmoil because they have access to cheaper & domestic WTI crude, while international competitors are stuck paying the Brent premium. As long as MPC can stay profitable and busy, MPLX’s Logistics and Storage segment remains a steady cash generator.

Other risks could include cost inflation as the Middle East conflict often spikes the price of steel and labor. If MPLX’s $2.4B growth projects see significant cost creep due to global supply chain disruptions, those mid-teens returners could actually be pressured. There could also be a case of demand destruction as if the Iran situation pushes US gasoline prices well above $4/gallon, a drop in domestic driving could slightly reduce the volumes through the company’s refined product pipelines.

I view MPLX and other large midstream companies as a geopolitical hedge, as they don’t have assets in the Persian Gulf but all across the US. When the Middle East becomes unstable as it is currently the case, the energy security premium flows directly into the US shale patch, making MPLX’s infrastructure more valuable.

Valuation

I believe that MPLX and the broad midstream industry are relatively cheap considering the company’s EV/EBITDA of 13.79, which is at the upper tier of the industry. The current yield is also fairly high at 6.88% over the past twelve months, offering a great opportunity cost for investors waiting to capitalize on the current energy dynamics. You might notice that these two metrics differ from the company’s historical average, as the yield is lower and the EV/EBITDA is higher, indicating that MPLX is undergoing a valuation expansion. Unitholders are no longer just buying MPLX for the check but more so for its stability and the 12.5% distribution growth guidance management provided for the next two years. I also believe that MPLX deserves a premium, considering the MPC golden anchor, as a massive portion of MPLX’s revenue is backed by minimum volume commitments from Marathon. This ensures some of the highest utilization rates and thus, some of the highest profitability metrics.

As the company owns the logistics feeding Marathon’s refineries, it is virtually impossible for a competitor to underbid them, as they would not be able to build a new pipe into an existing refinery footprint. That’s why I believe MPLX has one of the best moats in the industry outside of its large pipeline network.

Risks

The main risk would be interest rates & the yield competition as MLPs like MPLX are generally valued on their yield. If the Fed keeps interest rates higher for longer to combat energy-driven inflation from the Iran situation, risk-free treasuries become a more attractive alternative to MLPs. MPLX just refinanced $1.5B in debt that was previously locked in at a rather small interest rate of 1.75%. Replacing that with new debt at 5.3% to 6.1% increases interest expenses and can slightly eat into the cash available for distribution growth.

MPLX’s greatest strength is also its greatest vulnerability as Marathon is its parent company and largest customer. If refining margins collapse, MPC might reduce its refining runs. And though MPLX has MVCs that protect the operation in the short term, a prolonged downturn for Marathon would eventually limit MPLX’s growth potential and logistics revenue.

Conclusion

I believe that you should buy MPLX’s units even more now, considering the volatility that is coming to the industry. The yield and safety protect unitholders while also providing the ability to capitalize on the current tailwinds that are set to increase the prices of MPLX’s units. I keep my Strong Buy rating.

data.title: Virtus Reaves Utilities ETF Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

Utility stocks modestly underperformed the S&P 500® Index during the quarter. While accelerating electricity demand driven by artificial intelligence ('AI') deployment and data center expansion supported higher load forecasts and capital spending expectations across the sector, rising interest rates and a moderation in investor risk appetite toward AI-exposed names weighed on relative performance.

Performance Review

The Fund declined 4.91% in the quarter, underperforming the S&P 500® Utilities Index (Utilities Index). Over the last 12 months, the Fund returned 25.53% versus 16.04% for the Utilities Index.

The Fund paid a fourth-quarter distribution of $0.38 per share. Net asset value ('NAV') per share at quarter-end was $78.89 and the Fund's 30-Day SEC yield 1 was 1.45%.

Top Contributors to Performance

Constellation Energy's performance was driven by multiple catalysts, including robust power market fundamentals, regulatory progress towards closing the Calpine acquisition, and an improving policy outlook. Specifically, the market began pricing in a more constructive Federal Energy Regulation Commission stance regarding grid interconnection and contracting structures necessary to support hyperscale data center demand.

DTE Energy (DTE), Duke Energy (DUK), WEC Energy (WEC), and OGE Energy (OGE) were all top contributors, owing to trading in the Fund. Specifically, DTE and Duke were sold near quarter highs in October, OGE was sold in October and bought back lower in December, and WEC was purchased near quarter lows in December. All four utilities have improving growth outlooks, but volatility during the quarter offered trading opportunities.

Top Detractors from Performance

After strong performance in the first nine months of the year, Talen Energy and Vistra lagged during the fourth quarter as the market digested recent gains. We view this mean reversion as a function of broader sentiment shifts, specifically a cooling in risk appetite for AI-exposed infrastructure names, rather than idiosyncratic fundamental weakness.

NI Source executed a key strategic milestone by announcing a data center agreement with Amazon (AMZN), but it, too, was impacted by the moderation in AI-exposed names. Xcel Energy detracted from performance, giving back gains achieved following a favorable resolution for the Colorado Marshall fire at the end of the third quarter. The stock faced pressure from a confluence of headlines regarding Texas wildfire litigation and heightened regulatory activity.

Shares of PPL Electric Utilities (PPL) were negatively impacted by comments made by a utility commissioner on its recently filed general rate case.

Outlook

The fourth quarter concluded a strong year for equity markets, with performance increasingly concentrated in high-growth companies. AI continued to command investor focus, though defensive sectors, including utilities, lagged the broader market for both the quarter and the year.

While accelerating electricity demand driven by AI deployment and data center expansion supported higher load forecasts and capital spending expectations across the sector, rising interest rates and moderation in investor risk appetite weighed on relative performance.

Power continues to be the gating factor to AI expansion, driven by growing computational requirements and the significant energy needed to cool high-density data centers. The rapid adoption and deployment of advanced graphics processing units (GPUS) have increased data center power intensity requirements, while thermal output has driven higher cooling needs. Together, these factors continue to support increasing electricity load profiles and demand, requiring incremental generation-, transmission-, and infrastructure-related investment.

We believe that the power required to support AI represents a durable, long-term investment theme. At the same time, we remain attentive to broader market dynamics and the importance of balancing cyclical exposure with resilient, cash-generative businesses.

data.title: Skeena Resources Targets $450 Million For The Eskay Creek Gold/Silver Mine
data.description:
Market Maintains Its Enthusiasm for Skeena Resources: Justified

We're not straying one bit from the current enthusiasm surrounding the NYSE-listed shares of Skeena Resources Limited (SKE), as this is exactly how the market feels about the stock. Fully understanding the market's perspective, our Buy signal aligns with our previous article.

Progress at Eskay Creek and Investor Confidence

The desire to own Skeena shares is obvious – it doesn’t take much to see it. The transparency with which this Canadian company is advancing its project for a precious metals production facility at the Eskay Creek project in British Columbia's Golden Triangle region is evident, with strides being made at every step. In this update article, which follows up on our previous coverage, the momentum is confirmed in three key areas, each reflecting continuous progress: the company's growth and investor confidence; deeper insights into the company's health and strategy; and the market dynamics and surrounding exploration that are driving interest in the value of the Eskay Creek Project.

Statistics confirm this upward trend:

Since November 8, 2025, the publication date of our previous article, a remarkable development has taken place: Skeena shares have overtaken the VanEck Junior Gold Miners ETF (GDXJ) and are following the bullishness of the Gold Spot Price (XAUUSD:CUR). To keep the chart clear, we use gold exclusively as the benchmark. This is also because Eskay Creek is expected to consist of 70% gold and 30% silver. The GDXJ ETF serves as an index representing all stocks in the precious global metal exploration and mining sector.

From Our Previous Coverage

In our previous analysis, we upgraded Skeena Resources' NYSE-listed shares to “Buy”. We recognized the company's strong growth potential, particularly with regard to the Eskay Creek project, one of the most productive and cost-effective gold and silver mines in the world. Nevertheless, the shares remained undervalued.

The company's $750 million financing package and its inclusion in the S&P/TSX Composite provided the necessary investors’ confidence and liquidity for the continued development of Eskay Creek. At the same time, its commitment to sustainable practices and close collaboration with Indigenous communities, especially the Tahltan Nation, strengthened its long-term prospects.

With precious metal prices rising, Skeena was well-positioned for further growth. Volatility presented buying opportunities and increasingly reinforced our Buy signal.

About Skeena Resources Limited and its Flagship project Eskay Creek

In a very recent press release (Newsfile Corp. – February 24, 2026), highlighting interesting industry-wide activities in the Eskay Creek region of Skeena in the Golden Triangle, Newsfile Corp. announces:

Eskay Creek, which lies approximately 60 km east of Golden Girl, produced approximately 3.3 million ounces of gold and 160,000 ounces of silver between 1994 and 2008 (with an estimated 3.3 million ounces of gold, 88 million ounces of silver in reserves). It should be noted that Eskay Creek Mine has recently been permitted to re-commence mining operations - see Skeena Resources Ltd. ( SKE) news releases dated January 28, 2026, and February 3, 2026.

We also learned from Newsfile Corp. that the “Golden Girl” project acquired by Gold Runner is a large new gold-silver system measuring 12 km x 7 km, featuring a gold-rich core (11.28 g/t Au) surrounded by a silver-rich halo (3,262 g/t Ag). It is located in the Iskut River region of northwestern British Columbia, further explaining investor interest in the Eskay Creek area.

Skeena's Eskay Creek project consists of 51 mineral claims and eight mineral leases covering a total area of ​​approximately 7,666.02 hectares in the Golden Triangle region of British Columbia.

The future Eskay Creek mine is located 250 km from Stewart, BC and is expected to produce 260,000 ounces of gold and 7.65 million ounces of silver annually for 370,000 ounces of gold equivalent (“AuEq Oz”) annually at AISC of $687/AuEq Oz produced. The pre-production capital of C$713 million is fully covered by a financing package from Orion Resource Partners totaling US$750 million (approximately C$1 billion).

Gold Runner CEO Chris Wensley explained (Newsfile Corp. – February 24, 2026):

We are now fully funded to undertake and complete the 2026 exploration program on Golden Girl. […]. This is a tremendously exciting time for the Company and our team. We look forward to executing and delivering on our plan and bringing notable results to our shareholders.

If the results are notable for Gold Runner shareholders, Skeena's NYSE-listed shares will likely face the same expectations for the future development of Eskay Creek.

Road to 2027 Initial Production and Cash flow: Project Development and Permitting Updates

The Eskay Creek gold-silver project received Environmental Management Act (EMA) approval on February 3, 2026 – the final permit required to commence commercial mining in the second quarter of 2027. This was done in cooperation with the central government of Tahltan and followed the authorization under the British Columbia Mines Act (MA) of January 27, 2026.

Honourable Jagrup Brar, Minister of Mining and Critical Minerals, stated:

I congratulate Skeena on reaching this important milestone with the receipt of the final permit to commence full-scale construction at Eskay Creek. […]. We look forward to the positive contributions this project will bring to British Columbia’s mining sector and to the communities it supports.

On behalf of the company, Randy Reichert, CEO of Skeena, said:

[…] With this approval in hand, we are well-positioned to advance toward construction and long-term value creation for all stakeholders.

Also on January 27, 2026, Skeena received Environmental impact Assessment (EAC) certificate, concluding the comprehensive environmental impact assessment process that began in August 2024.

Skeena's consistent compliance with all regulatory requirements and its partnership with the Tahltan Nation are milestones in the lead-up to the resumption of mining at Eskay Creek.

Skeena Resources' Financial Situation: An Update as of September 30, 2025

Skeena Resources Limited is not currently producing any tradable quantities of precious metals and therefore generates no revenue or profit. The earnings releases show how the company is using the financing from the $750 million package to commence production at Eskay Creek in the second quarter of 2027. The next earnings release is expected to be released after market close on Monday, March 30, 2026. Until then, we must rely on Skeena Gold & Silver's financial results for the third quarter of 2025, which were released on November 13, 2025, as this is the most recent document available.

Cost Capitalization with Skeena Developer: Value Creation

By not producing payable metal, it is clear that Skeena has incurred losses of C$111.1 million in the nine months ended September 30, 2025 and C$36.8 million in the three months ended September 30, 2025. However, these losses were less severe than in the corresponding periods in 2024, reflecting a 25% improvement for the nine-month period and 57% for the quarter, signaling positive momentum as Skeena transitions from an exploration-focused company to a developer on track to become a producer by Q2 2027. This transition involves the capitalization of exploration and evaluation costs, which is why these expenses are remarkably lower. A key indicator of this shift is the decrease in operating expenses to C$56.5 million for 9M25 (down from C$86.1 million in 9M24), leading to a higher value for fixed assets or “Net Property, Plant & Equipment”.

But in this phase, where commodities, for example, are doing exceptionally well, the company has "fortunately" found some strength within itself, as Skeena skillfully manages the trading of marketable securities and squeezes a profit out of these short-term investment activities. But these are additional funds, as in this development phase, where the money from financing constructs the value of the assets that will produce the marketable metal, Skeena backs up the consumption of cash with the instruments of the Orion financing package.

Skeena Is Securing Additional Financing

As 9M25 demonstrates, the company is securing more financing, mainly from the gold stream and a bought deal financing, which are being reinvested in the Eskay Creek project. However, as the 9M25 numbers show, the company is obtaining more funding primarily from the gold stream and bought deal financings than is being reinvested in Eskay Creek. These are the earlier stages of the $750 million financing package before moving on to the secured loan (which will likely be secured by the value being built today); remember, net tangible assets amounted to C$327 million (or approx.. $240 million) as of September 30, 2025, and we are on track to matching the value of the $350 million in committed capital available from a senior secured loan. The financing started with initial available value of $300 million structured into equity investments worth $100 million and gold stream worth $200 million. We are well over halfway through the project's financing phase with regard to the next step of senior secured loans. For now, the funds from the gold stream and bought deal financing are more than sufficient to secure the investment during this operational phase: C$282.7 million, primarily from the gold stream and the equity investment agreement, compared to capital expenditures of C$215 million in the first nine months of the fiscal year 2025. This potentially generates a cash flow surplus of C$68 million, which can be carried forward for future activities. Furthermore, C$108.2 million in cash and cash equivalents were available as of September 30, 2025.

Most Recent Information on the Status of Construction Activities

Skeen Resources has financed the Eskay Creek project. Construction has been underway since 2025, and the screenshot shows the activity planned for the last quarter in 2025.

On Monday, March 30, 2026, the company will release its earnings report. This will include details on contract activities for 2026, the use of funds from the gold stream contract, and the expected receipt of the next tranche of the senior secured loan. Since the last financing round indicates that the work completed to date should secure access to another $350 million tranche (not including a potential $100 million cost overrun facility), this could be a more positive signal for Skeena's stock price on the NYSE. The loan comes at a critical time for the North American economy, as lenders remain cautious about lending.

SKE Skeena Resources Limited On The NYSE

As of this writing, shares of Skeena Resources Limited, listed on the NYSE under the symbol “SKE”, traded at $35.30 per share, representing a gain of 106.75%, outperforming the S&P 500's +0.12% since our last article’s Buy signal. The company's market capitalization is $4.56 billion.

Technical Analysis

The stock has traded between $8.53 and $38.77 per share over the past 52 weeks. The midpoint of this range is $23.65 per share, and the current stock price is 49.3% above this midpoint. Looking at the sequence of the moving average (MA) lines, the following picture emerges: the 20-day line is above the 50-day line. The 50-day line is above the 100-day line, and the 100-day line is above the 200-day line. The market is bullish in the short, medium, and long term, and there is no indication that it intends to abandon this position. This is understandable, as the company is making progress on its Eskay Creek project while precious metal prices are rising. It would be unusual if this development in Skeena's shares on the NYSE were not in line with the current trend in the company's activities. Despite this robust consensus among the MA lines on the upward trend, the RSI at 56.74 gives the stock price ample room to radiate its bullish tone with a stronger foothold and thrive on a sustainable long-term path that is constantly seeking new heights.

Valuation

Skeena Resources Limited website states:

Based on the Eskay Creek NI 43-101 Technical Report on Updated Feasibility Study (effective November 14, 2023), Eskay Creek will generate exceptional economics with attractive returns at prices of US$3,500 gold and US$40 silver:

Gold Spot Price (XAUUSD:CUR) is $5,097.88 per ounce, up 76.35% year-over-year and 18% year-to-date.

Silver Spot Price (XAGUSD:CUR) is $83.41 per ounce, up 162.76% year-over-year and 16.7% year-to-date.

Latest Forecasts:

To hedge against numerous uncertainties, ANZ Bank forecast a gold price of $5,800 per ounce for the second quarter of 2026, as reported by Seeking Alpha last month.

Bart Melek, global head of commodity strategy at TD Cowen, emphasized in an interview with CNBC, as Seeking Alpha has reported:

that silver’s ( XAGUSD:CUR) long-term outlook remains historically strong. […]. He forecasts an average price of around $65 for the year, which sits well above the cost curve, with expected supply deficits of approximately 80M ounces anticipated for the current year and potentially extending for several more years.

Eskay Creek has a net present value (NPV, 5%) of C$5.4 billion at a gold price of $3,500/oz and a silver price of $40/oz.

We perform the calculation assuming that the impact of gold and silver prices on the NPV is equally weighted.

The NPV is approximately C$9.6 billion at a gold price of $5,097.88/ounce and a silver price of $83.41/ounce.

The NPV is approximately C$8.9 billion at a gold price of $5,800/ounce and a silver price of $65/ounce.

Let's assume the NPV of Skeena's 100% stake in Eskay Creek is between $6.5 billion and $7 billion at the current Canadian-to-US-dollar exchange rate, compared to a market capitalization of $4.56 billion at the time of writing. This suggests a buy signal.

Or,

The annual free cash flow averages C$1 billion or $731.6 million, which corresponds to a price-to-FCF ratio of 6.2x compared to the sector median Price / Cash Flow (FWD) of 9.8x. This also supports buy signal.

Risks

These are the risks:

Financial Position Risk

The section “Skeena Resources' Financial Situation: An Update as of September 30, 2025” shows that Skeena Resources generated cash inflows of C$283 million from financing activities, while operating activities and capital expenditures absorbed C$272 million. The surplus of C$11.5 million is carried forward to the coming months. Together with cash and cash equivalents and short term investments of C$153 million, this results in a total of C$164.5 million. Based on the performance in the first nine months of 2025, this amount should be sufficient to cover the development of Eskay Creek for five to six months from September 30, 2025. Additional funding from the US$750 million Orion financing package, specifically the US$350 million secured loan, is not included in this calculation. The granting of final approval by British Columbia, which secures Skeena's progress at the Eskay Creek mine, has significantly reduced project risk. This means that there should be no further obstacles to the disbursement of funds from the senior secured loan. Furthermore, a C$100 million cost overrun facility is available. Sufficient liquidity should be in place until first commercial production and initial cash flow in the second quarter of 2027.

Furthermore, a $100 million cost overrun facility is available. Sufficient liquidity should be in place until first commercial production and initial cash flow in the second quarter of 2027.

Risk of Falling Gold and Silver Prices

The price of the precious metal has recently weakened somewhat, as the US dollar is seen as a safe haven from inflation fears in light of rising energy prices resulting from tensions in the Middle East. These tensions escalated with the US and Israeli attack on Iranian nuclear facilities, as the Islamic Republic is perceived as a threat should it continue uranium enrichment for the development of nuclear weapons. The market therefore expects interest rates to remain unchanged for the time being in light of renewed inflationary pressures, which will temporarily affect the price of the precious metal, since neither gold, nor silver yields interest. So far, however, the precious metal has benefited from a long-term demand factor: global turmoil and geopolitical tensions, which have led investors to use gold and silver as a hedge. We assess the risk of falling gold and silver prices due to an easing of global tensions as low. The tensions in the Middle East create additional uncertainty, enhance risk aversion, and lead investors to rely even more heavily on gold and silver as a hedge. Once this temporary phase is over, the bullion will be back to the uptrend mode.

Low Trading Volume Risk

According to Seeking Alpha’s “ Trading Data” section, only 1.02 million shares of Skeena Resource Limited have traded in the last three months, indicating low daily trading volume. Consequences: If a downtrend necessitates rapid liquidation of positions, the low trading volume forces sellers to accept significantly lower prices, often resulting in substantial capital losses. However, this structurally low liquidity also acts as a driver for higher multiples: If a large number of market participants suddenly wish to acquire shares of Skeena Resources, the limited share supply could trigger an aggressive and disproportionate improvement in the asset's market valuation.

Conclusion

Skeena Resources Limited is developing its flagship Eskay Creek project in British Columbia's Golden Triangle. The mine is projected to produce 260,000 ounces of gold and 7.65 million ounces of silver annually. The pre-production capital of C$713 million is fully covered by a $750 million (approximately C$1 billion) financing package from Orion Resource Partners. The company has received final regulatory approval from British Columbia for mine construction, significantly reducing risk in light of the additional $350 million secured senior debt financing. Initial profitable production is expected in the second quarter of 2027.
